The sample complexity for learning with gradient descent is determined by the landscape of the empirical loss.
The empirical loss landscape showcases rich behavior in the setting of learning a single-index model.
Single index models are target functions of the form f∗(x) = σ(w⋆ · x), where w⋆ ∈ Sd−1 is the unknown relevant direction and σ is the known link function.
The shape of the loss landscape is governed by the information exponent k⋆ of the link function σ, which characterizes the curvature of the loss landscape around the origin.
Ben Arous et al. [1] show that online stochastic gradient descent on the empirical loss can recover w∗ with n ≳ dmax(1,k⋆−1) samples.
Gradient descent can be suboptimal for various statistical learning problems, as it only relies on local information in the loss landscape and is thus prone to getting stuck in local minima.

The concept of local minima is related to the loss landscape as it describes the points in the loss function where the gradient is zero. The loss landscape is prone to getting stuck in local minima, affecting the optimization process.

The Correlational Statistical Query (CSQ) lower bound is applicable for learning a single index model and requires fewer samples compared to other methods. This relation highlights the importance of CSQ in the context of learning single index models.

There exists a gap between gradient-based methods and the CSQ lower bound in terms of sample requirements for various problems such as Tensor PCA. This gap signifies the difference in efficiency between these methods.

The gradient-based algorithm faces challenges in matching the CSQ lower bound for learning single-index models as discussed in reference [1]. The comparison highlights the limitations of gradient-based approaches in certain scenarios.

SGD is considered as a discretization of gradient flow in certain regimes, but recent work has shown that SGD has additional implicit regularization effects. This relationship indicates the evolving understanding of SGD's behavior relative to gradient flow.

Partial trace tensor Mn is defined by contracting indices of T until all that remains is a vector (if k is odd) or a matrix (if k is even).
When k⋆ is odd, Mn is directly returned as the estimate for w⋆. When k⋆ is even, the top eigenvector of Mn is returned.
